text:

Members of Goulburn's Thompson household could hardly watch as Olympic equestrian, Chris Burton claimed a silver medal on Monday, July 30.

Loginor signup to continue reading

His win behind Germany's Michael Jung in the individual eventing at the picturesque Chateau de Versailles course brought tears of joy to Burton and wife, Rebekah.

"It's amazing. It's a dream come true. I don't know what happens when you get old, you get emotional all of a sudden," Burton told media.

Sons Harry and Jack walked around proudly with their fathers' medal around their neck.

A world away in Goulburn, Rebekah's parents, Stewart and Jane Thompson were glued to their television screen.

Jane had to leave the room during tense moments as Burton competed in the final showjumping round. Both watched as their son-in-law lost just 0.4 penalty points to claim the silver medal.

"We're very excited for Chris because we know how much work he puts in to get to where he is...We're very proud of him," Mr Thompson said.

The couple is eagerly anticipating Chris, Rebekah and family's permanent return to Australia in December. They aim to establish an equestrian centre at the Thompsons' property on Goulburn's outskirts.

Burton went into Monday in third position following the dressage and cross country but moved up in the ranks following the showjumping qualifying round. Mr Thompson said his borrowed horse,Shadow Man, had captured his attention just five months ago and the pair "gelled."

The Olympian had given eventing away several years ago to concentrate on showjumping but returned in March and climbed up the rankings during numerous European competitions.

The silver medal adds to the team bronze he won at the Rio Olympics in 2016. It was his third Olympic Games.

Burton is no stranger to Goulburn. He was a regular competitor at the annualLynton Horse Trialson Range Road before leaving for the UK some 15 years ago.

Organiser, Neil Kennedy, said the 42-year-old Burton competed there from his early twenties.

"He is one of the top riders in the world and could have been on the Olympic showjumping team," he said.

"He is a brilliant jockey. Others compete on the same level but there are light years between them and him. Chris is a very nice individual and is humble about his abilities...I'm thrilled to see him win silver."

Burton, 42, grew up on a grain and cattle farm at Brymaroo, west of Toowoomba.

Mr Thompson speculated Chris achieved his balance from riding on the back of a motorbike with his brother, Carl, while carrying two school bags. He started riding horses from young, undoubtedly encouraged by his mother who played polocrosse for Queensland.

Rebekah too honed her horse skills from a young age, including at Brealbane Pony Club.

Burton met Rebekah at equestrian competitions at which both competed but their relationship rekindled at a horse clinic at Wallaby Hills, Robertson in about 2011.

In 2012 they moved to England where they ran an eventing business. Chris also completed throughout Europe.

They married in December, 2015 at the Thompson property.

In 2019 the couple and their young sons moved to Dorset, Surrey, where Chris helped design and build a premier equestrian centre at Chedington Estate owned by Dr Geoffrey and Kate Guy. There, the Burtons also run an eventing business. Rebekah, equipped with a law and science degree, is hands on and drives large horse floats around the countryside.

They hope to start a similar operation on Rebekah's family's property when they return home with a few horses in tow later in the year.

"We're excited about them coming home," Mr Thompson said.

For now they're delighting in his Olympic success and the realisation of a long held dream.

title: Chris Burton's Olympic silver medal delights Goulburn family | Goulburn Post | Goulburn, NSW
description: Goulburn couple, Stewart and Jane Thompson, cheered on son-in-law Chris Burton has he claimed and Olympic silver medal in individual eventing.
